original_abstract: Unveiling the nature of active sites under realistic reaction conditions is a fundamental challenge in the development of efficient single-atom catalysts (SACs). In this study, we utilize a model catalyst consisting of iron phthalocyanine immobilized on carbon nanotubes (FePc-CNT) to investigate the structural and electronic dynamics of atomically dispersed iron centers during the electrochemical CO2 reduction reaction (CO2RR). By integrating operando $^{57}$Fe Mössbauer, X-ray absorption (XAS), and Raman spectroscopies, we track the evolution of the iron sites from their initial high-spin (HS) $O_2$-Fe(III)N$_4$ resting state. Our findings demonstrate that under cathodic potential, the iron center undergoes a reduction to a HS Fe(II)N$_4$ state. Crucially, we observe distinct behaviors depending on the electrolyte environment: while the centers are prone to irreversible reduction to metallic iron under Ar, the presence of CO$_2$ stabilizes the site through the formation of a HS Fe(II)Pc$^–$-CO$_2^–$ intermediate. This species involves a one-electron reduction of the phthalocyanine ligand rather than the metal center itself. Theoretical calculations support these experimental observations, revealing that the electroreduction of the Pc ligand shifts the d-band center of the iron, optimizing the binding strength of CO$_2$ and significantly enhancing catalytic turnover. This work provides a comprehensive atomic-level picture of the synergistic role between metal centers and organic ligands in SACs, offering critical insights for the rational design of CO2RR electrocatalysts.
humanized_abstract: The active site structure under working conditions of single-atom catalysts is unclear, which greatly hampers their design. Using iron phthalocyanine immobilized on carbon nanotubes (FePc-CNT) as a model catalyst, we explore the structure evolution of atomically dispersed iron species under working conditions for the electrochemical reduction of carbon dioxide (CO2RR) using operando 57Fe Mössbauer, X-ray absorption (XAS) and Raman spectroscopies. The iron centers are confirmed to be in the high-spin (HS) O2-Fe(III)N4 species, and they are reduced to the HS Fe(II)N4 species at the cathodic potential. Importantly, the iron centers can be reduced to metallic iron irreversibly under Ar condition, while the CO2 condition facilitates the activation of iron centers into the HS Fe(II)Pc−-CO2− species, involving a one-electron reduction of the Pc ligand, rather than that of the iron center. These experimental observations are further confirmed by theoretical calculations, showing that the electroreduction of the Pc ligand increases the d-band center of iron and thus enhances the binding of CO2, which significantly increases the activity of the iron sites. This work provides a new atomic-scale picture for understanding the cooperation between active metal centers and organic ligands in single-atom catalysts and gives an important clue for designing new efficient electrocatalysts for CO2RR.

The iron centers are confirmed to be in the high-spin (HS) O2-Fe(III)N4 species, and they are reduced to the HS Fe(II)N4 species at the cathodic potential.
